author: aditya24raj

body:
linuxdeploy can package required libs automatically. but some are not detected by it as they are loaded only at runtime. so we do iterative workflow. run the appimage see which missing file it is complaining about and package them in appimage. and repeat until no errors.

those extra files where selected because of that iterative workflow. first it compalined about libgobject, then libpango and then libpangoft.

libgobject was special case. error said something relating to invalid lookup g_uri_ref. turns out the appimage was loading libglib from appimage but libgobject from host. this mismatch was causing this error. so it was packaged with appimage
author: aditya24raj

body:
with AppImageKit, everything thing has to be done manually, as i remember. make directory structure, place executables and necessary libs.
with linuxdeploy we give it executable path with -e flag, and it packages executable, along with necessary libs(to some extent).
so for simple stuff we can do manually with AppImageKit but for anything else linuxdeploy provides better experience.
linuxdeploy is also mentioned on offical documentation for usage.
https://docs.appimage.org/packaging-guide/from-source/linuxdeploy-user-guide.html

linuxdeploy is also recommended for packaging native binaries.
https://docs.appimage.org/packaging-guide/from-source/native-binaries.html

Btw, isn't there an official debian/ubuntu package for these tools?
i don't think so. most of their tools are in .appimage format itself.

i''m open for suggestions to use any other packaging tool too.

body:
Unlike what would be expected, adb shell just concatenates the arguments as strings same
as if they were just separated by spaces. Sending the commands in one argument
or sending as multiple arguments doesn't preduce any different outcome.
Sad but that's how adb works.

try typing this in your local shell (you may need the `-s` argument for adb):
`adb shell 'am broadcast'`
vs
`adb shell am broadcast`

If they both work, means there's no reasonable way to deliver the arguments as-is, even when using `execvp` (which scrcpy is using).
Sadly, they have to be escaped.

Whether the escape be done here or before the calling stack to call adb shell is up for debate. However, no doubt it's needed and no doubt the behavior adb has is improper when intending to provide arguments as-is
author: brunoais

body:
> Unlike what would be expected, adb shell just concatenates the arguments as strings same
as if they were just separated by spaces

In fact, it depends on the adb version and the platform. It's a total mess. You can't rely on any specific behavior here. Adding quotes might break on some platforms and some adb versions.

Anyway, in practice, the command line should not exceed 256 chars (it [crashes](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/issues/2841#issuecomment-984425680) on some devices), so the script content could not be passed as a server argument.

body:
These images are generated and maintained by Circle CI, a provider of CI tooling & infrastructure.  I had a look at the container images which can be used for building Android apps, and this seemed to be one of the most maintained ones.  The source repo is here: https://github.com/CircleCI-Public/cimg-android.

That's a long way to say I think they are fairly trustworthy.  I tried to start from ubuntu:jammy and then install ADK from the Ubuntu package repositories, but the ADK that ships with Ubuntu is too old.  The other option is to start from a vanilla ubuntu:jammy image (or the vanilla image for your favorite distro) and manually install the ADK.  Or not run in a container at all, but directly on the GitHub actions VM, which ship with the ADK as well: https://github.com/actions/runner-images/blob/main/images/linux/Ubuntu2204-Readme.md#android.

In general, I think you're better of using containers because it shields you from any changes on the underlying build machines.

For the app, I would recommend running the build for the app in a separate job in a separate container, as the build dependencies are quite different: the server requires the ADK, the app itself requires headers for libav*, libusb, mingw and a bunch of other dependencies.

I originally had a job which would build the client as well, but then decided against it as I didn't have the time to fully complete that work (done is better than perfect, I guess).

body:
Currently, the AOA/HID keyboard handles two different things:
 1. create the HID events
 2. send them over AOA

To reuse the HID part but using another protocol, your PR abstracts (2) (how to send them):
 - introduce a new trait `sc_hid_interface`, implemented by `sc_aoa` and `sc_uhid`
 - an instance is passed to construct a `sc_hid_keyboard`

This works, but it brings some complexity:
 - two levels of interfaces implementations (something implementing a `sc_key_processor`, with one implementation receiving something implementing a `sc_hid_interface`)
 - the need for an `async_message` flag (because not all implementations behave exactly the same)
 - all implementation have to `create()`/`destroy()` on the client-side (for UHID, I would prefer to create the UHID struct on the server side, the client should not have to handle all these details, like `vendor_id`/`product_id`, and the UHID handle should probably be closed on exit from the server side, without any message from the client)

I think "abstracting" the other way around ((1) extracting how to create the HID events) leads to a simpler solution (with less coupling).

My idea is to extract a component to create HID events (regardless of how they will be sent), so that separate keyboard implementations could reuse that same HID tools (without interfaces/traits) when they need to.

That way, we could get 3 separate keyboard/mouse "independant" implementations:
 - `sc_keyboard_sdk` (previously `sc_keyboard_inject")
 - `sc_keyboard_aoa` (previously `sc_hid_keyboard`)
 - `sc_keyboard_uhid`

Concretly:
 - `app/src/hid` would contain HID tools
 - `app/src/usb` would contain the AOA keyboard/mouse implementations
 - `app/src/uhid` would contain the UHID keyboard/mouse implementations

I implemented preliminary commits which perform all these refactors with the current features: [`uhid.10`](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/commits/uhid.10/)

From this branch, you could more easily add a UHID implementation (independently of the AOA keyboard).

What do you think?
author: rom1v

body:
This is a structure compiled for the computer architecture, it should not be used as is to pass it to the device architecture (in theory, endianness could be different for example).

IMO, the client should only send the descriptor, and the device construct the expected uhid structure on the server side.
author: rom1v

body:
> IMO, the client should only send the descriptor, and the device construct the expected uhid structure on the server side.

I'd like letting the client to specify device names (like `Scrcpy UHID mouse`/`Scrcpy UHID keyboard`), because the server doesn't know the device's type.

Client also needs to be able to specify `vendor` and `product`, because my gamepad rumble implementation only works with a specific pair of those (by emulating a Bluetooth Xbox One controller to load a special driver).

Because little-endian is so dominant on modern PC and Android, I don't know whether I need to deal with endianness even if those structures are created in Java. The header file is copied from Linux kernel so using C to handle them is the simplest way.
author: yume-chan

body:
> I'd like letting the client to specify device names (like Scrcpy UHID mouse/Scrcpy UHID keyboard), because the server doesn't know the device's type.

Sure. I'd pass all the necessary fields in a control message structure, with serialization/deserialization ((like the other control messages).
author: rom1v

body:
> I don't know whether I need to deal with endianness even if those structures are created in Java.

I guess so, you must pass a `byte[]` with the expected representation to your `UHidManager.write()` method.
You should probably use a `ByteBuffer` to build it (ref `ByteBuffer.order(…)`, `ByteOrder.nativeOrder()`).

To explain a bit more, I think it is incorrect that the client knows the UHID implementation details (including endianness, some fields that the client does not care about…). Moreover, it is possible to use scrcpy-server standalone (and control it from python for example). Even if we do not absolutely need to consider this usage, it still seems weird to expect to pass a Linux C-struct for UHID messages.

cd "$DEPS_DIR"
. common

VERSION=2.30.7
VERSION=2.30.8
FILENAME=SDL-$VERSION.tar.gz
PROJECT_DIR=SDL-release-$VERSION
SHA256SUM=1578c96f62c9ae36b64e431b2aa0e0b0fd07c275dedbc694afc38e19056688f5
SHA256SUM=380C295EA76B9BD72D90075793971C8BCB232BA0A69A9B14DA4AE8F603350058

cd "$SOURCES_DIR"

if [[ -d "$PROJECT_DIR" ]]
then
    echo "$PWD/$PROJECT_DIR" found
else
    get_file "https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/archive/refs/tags/release-$VERSION.tar.gz" "$FILENAME" "$SHA256SUM"
    tar xf "$FILENAME"  # First level directory is "$PROJECT_DIR"
fi
mkdir -p "$BUILD_DIR/$PROJECT_DIR"
cd "$BUILD_DIR/$PROJECT_DIR"
export CFLAGS='-O2'
export CXXFLAGS="$CFLAGS"
if [[ -d "$HOST" ]]
then
    echo "'$PWD/$HOST' already exists, not reconfigured"
    cd "$HOST"
else
    mkdir "$HOST"
    cd "$HOST"
    "$SOURCES_DIR/$PROJECT_DIR"/configure \
        --prefix="$INSTALL_DIR/$HOST" \
        --host="$HOST_TRIPLET" \
        --enable-shared \
        --disable-static
fi
make -j
# There is no "make install-strip"
make install
# Strip manually
${HOST_TRIPLET}-strip "$INSTALL_DIR/$HOST/bin/SDL2.dll"

There is only one virtual display per scrcpy session, do we need one input port per UHID device?

I think we could just use `String inputPort = "scrcpy:" + Os.getpid();` and call `addUniqueIdAssociationByPort()` only once (and all UHID devices would use the same input port associated to the single display id). That way, storing an inputPort per device would be unnecessary.

I'm not asking you to make any change, I would just like to know what you think about it.

According to the [migration guide](https://wiki.libsdl.org/SDL3/README-migration), near the bottom of the page:
```
The following window operations are now considered to be asynchronous requests and should not be
assumed to succeed unless a corresponding event has been received:

  - SDL_SetWindowSize() (SDL_EVENT_WINDOW_RESIZED)
  - SDL_SetWindowPosition() (SDL_EVENT_WINDOW_MOVED)
  - SDL_MinimizeWindow() (SDL_EVENT_WINDOW_MINIMIZED)
  - SDL_MaximizeWindow() (SDL_EVENT_WINDOW_MAXIMIZED)
  - SDL_RestoreWindow() (SDL_EVENT_WINDOW_RESTORED)
  - SDL_SetWindowFullscreen() (SDL_EVENT_WINDOW_ENTER_FULLSCREEN / SDL_EVENT_WINDOW_LEAVE_FULLSCREEN)

If it is required that operations be applied immediately after one of the preceding calls, the
SDL_SyncWindow() function will attempt to wait until all pending window operations have completed. 
```

Right after calling `SDL_SetWindowFullscreen()` you toggle `screen->fullscreen` and render the screen, so I'm just wondering if that's still a valid way of doing things, considering asynchronous operations?

body:
```suggestion
```

I scratched my head when reading https://wiki.libsdl.org/SDL3/README-main-functions#the-main-entry-point-in-sdl3 but now I think I understand the global picture.

In SDL2:
- `SDL.h` includes `SDL_main.h` [here](https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/blob/SDL2/include/SDL.h#L31).
- `SDL_main.h` checks for a defined symbol `SDL_MAIN_HANDLED` to avoid redefining the `main` function [here](https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/blob/SDL2/include/SDL_main.h#L33).
- Thus scrcpy has to define `SDL_MAIN_HANDLED` because it includes `SDL.h` which includes `SDL_main.h`.
- This is important when it is linked to the `SDLmain` static library which provides the real `main` entry point.
- `SDL.h` includes `SDL_stdinc.h` [here](https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/blob/SDL2/include/SDL.h#L32).
- `SDL_stdinc.h` does not check `SDL_FUNCTION_POINTER_IS_VOID_POINTER` nor does it define `SDL_FunctionPointer`.

In SDL3:
- `SDL.h` does *not* include `SDL_main.h` [here](https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/blob/main/include/SDL3/SDL.h#L33).
- `SDL_main.h` checks for a defined symbol `SDL_MAIN_HANDLED` to avoid redefining the `main` function [here](https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/blob/main/include/SDL3/SDL_main.h#L147).
- Thus scrcpy does not need to define `SDL_MAIN_HANDLED` because it includes `SDL.h` but which does not include `SDL_main.h`.
- This is on purpose since the `SDLmain` static library has been replaced by a single-header library (`SDL_main.h` does the whole work now).
- `SDL.h` includes `SDL_stdinc.h` [here](https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/blob/main/include/SDL3/SDL.h#L34).
- `SDL_stdinc.h` checks `SDL_FUNCTION_POINTER_IS_VOID_POINTER` [here](https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/blob/main/include/SDL3/SDL_stdinc.h#L6156) in order to define `SDL_FunctionPointer`.
- Hence scrcpy has to define `SDL_FUNCTION_POINTER_IS_VOID_POINTER` so as to work around a compiler error somewhere (right?).
- However scrcpy does not need to explicitly include `SDL_stdinc.h` because it is already included by `SDL.h`.

body:
I fixed it in a separate commit for clarity: e24018b4b7fdc3e18eb40d0f958916536358c989

I completely removed the local fields. Even if they were only updated on SDL events, their state was fundamentally inconistent. For example:
 1. start in windowed mode
 2. maximize the window → MAXIMIZED event
 3. enable fullscreen → RESTORED event then ENTER_FULLSCREEN event
 4. disable fullscreen → LEAVE_FULLSCREEN event then MAXIMIZED event

If the `maximized` field was updated on `MAXIMIZED`, then its value would be `false` in `LEAVE_FULLSCREEN`. But `SDL_GetWindowFlags()` still returns a `MAXIMIZED` state (the internal state is set before the event is posted, but the boolean field can only be updated when the event is handled).

So far, I only tested on Linux+X11. The relevant use case is:
 1. start in windowed mode
 2. maximize the window
 3. enable fullscreen
 4. rotate the device (switch portrait/landscape)
 5. disable fullscreen
 6. unmaximize

The resulting window should be resized to the new rotated dimensions.

body:
## Pull request overview This PR introduces a new “stream sink” output path to stream the device’s video/audio as MPEG-TS over a user-provided URL, integrating it into scrcpy’s CLI/options and packet pipeline. **Changes:** - Add a new `sc_stream_sink` component (`stream_sink.c/.h`) implementing `sc_packet_sink` for video/audio and handling client connections/output streaming. - Wire the stream sink into `scrcpy.c` initialization/cleanup and expose it via a new `--stream-sink` CLI option plus `scrcpy_options.stream_sink`. - Increase `SC_PACKET_SOURCE_MAX_SINKS` to allow one more packet sink. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 8 out of 8 changed files in this pull request and generated 7 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | app/src/trait/packet_source.h | Increase max packet sinks to allow adding the new stream sink. | | app/src/stream_sink.h | New public interface/type definitions for the stream sink component. | | app/src/stream_sink.c | New streaming implementation: URL handling, template ctx setup, client threads, fan-out queues. | | app/src/scrcpy.c | Initialize/start/stop/join/destroy the stream sink and add it as a packet sink. | | app/src/options.h | Add `stream_sink` option field. | | app/src/options.c | Default `stream_sink` to NULL. | | app/src/cli.c | Add `--stream-sink` CLI flag and adjust auto-disable logic for video/audio. | | app/meson.build | Build the new `stream_sink.c` source file. |
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: stream_sink.c calls snprintf() in sc_url_append_param(), but <stdio.h> is not included in this file. With C99+ (and typical -Werror=implicit-function-declaration), this will fail to compile. Include <stdio.h> in stream_sink.c (or include a header that declares snprintf).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: sc_stream_sink_interrupt_cb() and sc_stream_sink_client_interrupt_cb() read sink->stopped/client->stopped without holding the mutex, but these flags are also written from other threads. In C this is a data race (UB). Consider making these flags atomic_bool (as done elsewhere in the codebase) or otherwise ensuring atomic/locked access; the interrupt callback must not take a blocking lock.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: sc_stream_sink_is_connectionless() only treats udp:// as connectionless, but the surrounding comments and sc_stream_sink_build_connect_url() also mention/support rtp://. As written, rtp:// URLs will go through the connection-oriented accept loop, which is likely incorrect for RTP. Extend the check to cover rtp:// (or adjust the comments/logic accordingly).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The doc comment for sc_stream_sink_build_connect_url() claims that for srt:// it adds both mode=listener and latency=50 if not already set, but the implementation currently only sets mode=listener. Either update the comment to match current behavior or implement the latency default as documented.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: On avformat_write_header() failure, the code sets client_ctx->pb = NULL and frees the context, but it never closes the AVIOContext for non-SRT protocols. This leaks the socket/FD and associated buffers. Close pb (or avio_closep) on failure for protocols where it is safe, and avoid unconditionally nulling pb before cleanup.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The SRT disconnect workaround nulls ctx->pb and skips avio_close(). However, elsewhere in the codebase the pattern is to explicitly avio_close() before avformat_free_context(); if pb is NULL, avformat_free_context() will not close it, which risks leaking sockets when clients reconnect repeatedly. If avio_close() is unsafe for SRT, consider an alternative shutdown/close strategy that still releases the AVIOContext resources.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: sc_stream_sink_destroy() does not clear/destroy the sink-level video_queue/audio_queue. If the sink is stopped before template_ready (or init fails), these queues may still contain AVPacket refs, leaking packets and the vecdeque backing arrays. Clear pending packets and call sc_vecdeque_destroy() for both queues during destroy (after the thread is joined).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

The question is whether you want to enforce respecting `--min-size-alignment` or not, since [it can be higher than the codec alignment](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/blob/175134c0ca1d9018c14d10b467dbfcdff6d1c3be/server/src/main/java/com/genymobile/scrcpy/video/SurfaceEncoder.java#L61-L66) (and minimum size).

I can set `--min-size-alignment=16`, however it would still crash with a display size < 16 if we don't enforce the alignment of `minCodecSize`.

Even if, as you say we can assume, the video capabilities are consistent (i.e. correctly reported codec limits and alignment), it is possible that the user sets a `--min-size-alignment` higher than the codec alignment.

Before the flex display feature, `virtualDisplay` was only accessed from the `SurfaceEncoder` thread, so synchronization was not needed.

Now, the controller thread must retrieve the current `virtualDisplay`, so it needs synchronization.

The `virtualDisplay` field can only be written from the `SurfaceEncoder` thread. This write needs synchronization because the controller thread can read it (also with synchronization). However, reads from the `SurfaceEncoder` thread do not need synchronization, since read/writes occur on the same thread.

body:
We can think about it differently: When can/should `triggerResize()` be called? And we can also check the Android source code.

- Calls to `virtualDisplay.getDisplay().getDisplayId()` are safe because these methods are only thin wrappers around final fields:
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/VirtualDisplay.java;l=51-53
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/VirtualDisplay.java;l=37
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/view/Display.java;l=742-744
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/view/Display.java;l=105

- Calls to `setSurface()`, `resize()`, and `release()` are internally synchronized (if my little research is correct; please verify!):
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/VirtualDisplay.java;l=69-112
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/DisplayManagerGlobal.java;l=905-928
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/IDisplayManager.aidl;l=111-119
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/DisplayManagerService.java;l=5152-5188
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/DisplayManagerService.java;l=2304-2340
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/DisplayManagerService.java;l=249-260
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/DisplayManagerService.java;l=481-482
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/VirtualDisplayAdapter.java;l=231-281
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/VirtualDisplayAdapter.java;l=560-584
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/VirtualDisplayAdapter.java;l=531-545
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/DisplayDevice.java;l=396-415
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/view/SurfaceControl.java;l=4212-4225
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/view/SurfaceControl.java;l=4143-4161
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/view/SurfaceControl.java;l=217-218
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/view/SurfaceControl.java;l=207-208

- Therefore there is no need to synchronize `virtualDisplay.setSurface(surface)`.

- So the issue was not concurrent mutation _per se_, but rather, the fact that we can't resize the virtual display after it is released. More precisely, we can't do anything with it because at this point `IVirtualDisplayCallback` is null:
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/VirtualDisplay.java;l=110
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/VirtualDisplay.java;l=97
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/services/core/java/com/android/server/display/DisplayManagerService.java;l=5161 -> NPE

- Technically, however, if we would allow `release()` and `resize()` to be called concurrently, they should still be synchronized because of the `mToken` field in `VirtualDisplay` (or is it not a problem?):
  - https://cs.android.com/android/platform/superproject/+/android-16.0.0_r4:frameworks/base/core/java/android/hardware/display/VirtualDisplay.java;l=110

- But a better approach would thus be to _not_ allow to call `resize()` after `release()` even without explicit synchronization. If I'm right this can be readily achieved by calling `debouncer.join()` (new method to join the debouncer thread) right after `debouncer.stop()` (or just adding `thread.join()` inside `DisplayResizeDebouncer.stop()`). Hence this would ensure that `triggerResize()` is finished before `virtualDisplay.release()` runs. The potential overhead for joining the thread is negligible since this happens when scrcpy is closing, anyway.

- Another negligible cost is if you synchronize `virtualDisplay = ServiceManager.getDisplayManager().createNewVirtualDisplay()` locally instead of having a setter just for changing the reference (personally I would find it a bit more readable and easier for local reasoning). Putting `createNewVirtualDisplay()` inside a synchronized block will not introduce delay because `startNew()` is called only once, at the first encoding session, and `triggerResize()` is not supposed to be called before the virtual display is created.

- But the question is whether synchronization is even needed in `startNew()`. I don't think so but I may be wrong. Since the `virtualDisplay` reference never really changes, it only goes from null -> set -> null, as long as `triggerResize()` checks `if (virtualDisplay != null)` it will only work with the same non-null reference (provided we keep this method synchronized with the release or we join the debouncer thread). The only thing is that it could read an outdated null `virtualDisplay` (e.g. stale cached value). I should check the JVM memory model but from what I remember, either `triggerResize()` reads a null value and returns, or reads a non-null reference to a perfectly valid instance. … Hum, I did some reading and obviously [I was wrong](https://stackoverflow.com/a/13033512). Perhaps a `volatile virtualDisplay` would be sufficient. This would require further research.

- However, `triggerResize()` should _not_ even be called before the virtual display is created, otherwise that would mean discarding early resize requests (the requested size would be lost, right?). This doesn't seem dramatic as the user can re-resize if needed. And with the 300ms debounce timer, this is very improbable. But still, it would be better if we could programmatically guarantee that `triggerResize()` cannot be called after `release()` (as explained above) nor before `createNewVirtualDisplay()` (otherwise `triggerResize()` returns early, which discards the new size), and also, I think, **not before `displayMonitor.start()` is done** (otherwise `triggerResize()` could call `virtualDisplay.resize()` before the listener is attached, which would prevent resetting the session).

- Maybe there could be a way to avoid discarding early requests, e.g.:
  ```java
      private synchronized void triggerResize(Size size) {
          if (virtualDisplay != null) {
              […]
              virtualDisplay.resize([…]);
          } else {
              debouncer.requestResize(size);
          }
  ```
  But I don't know if this would even work and it seems to be a non-critical corner case.

---

So I see three ways going forward, please tell me if I'm mistaken or if you see another way:
1. Keep `triggerResize()` and `virtualDisplay.release()` + `virtualDisplay = null` synchronized, but maybe remove the setter and instead synchronize `virtualDisplay = ServiceManager.getDisplayManager().createNewVirtualDisplay()` directly.
2. Remove every synchronization, make `virtualDisplay` volatile, and join the debouncer thread in `release()` before releasing the virtual display.
3. Like point 2 but in addition replace `if (virtualDisplay != null)` by `assert virtualDisplay != null` in `triggerResize()` and also make sure this method can't be called before the virtual display is created (this would _de facto_ avoid discarding early resize requests).

I'm still concerned with `displayMonitor.start()` that could technically be called after `virtualDisplay.resize()`. Do you think it's possible and would it be an issue? An option could be to synchronize it in the same block as  `virtualDisplay = ServiceManager.getDisplayManager().createNewVirtualDisplay()` (assuming the setter is removed), another option would be to keep the setter and move `setCurrentVirtualDisplay(vd);` after `displayMonitor.start()` (i.e., at the very end of `startNew()`).

body:
`--max-size` can be useful with `--flex-display`, for instance:
- If you know that you can't stream with a too big display size because you would have too much delay or performance issues (but you still want  to keep high resolution etc. for smaller sizes).
- If like with my emulator the limits provided by the encoder are wrong, you can force a manual limit such that it doesn't crash.

In practice, there is a maximum possible size already (the encoder constraints). If you try to resize the window to a larger size, the virtual display will just remain at the maximum possible size. The suggested change fixes a wrong `can_resize` value when manually resizing in landscape orientation.

This explains another resize glitch that I observed on Wayland only in landscape mode, not in portrait. I initially thought it was simply due to my emulator slowness.

The effect was that when there are interleaved programmatic and _continuous_ manual resizing events, the window was resized to the new display size, and this was visible because the window was already manually resized to a slightly smaller/larger size at this point. In other words, there were little jumps when resizing in landscape on Wayland.

Technically, **this fix also solves [issue 3 on X11](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/pull/6772#issuecomment-4337803046) _for scrcpy_**. This is not strictly the exact same problem as issue 3 because X11 sends new size values out of nowhere when you try to resize the window with the new display size, thus the visual glitch is much more pronounced. But with this change, we no longer apply the new display size since it originated from a client resize, so the issue is bypassed. In general though, I could reproduce the X11 issue with a minimal sample code with interleaved programmatic and manual resizes. Not scrcpy's business, anyway. I might open an issue in SDL repo later. See here: https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/issues/15532

Please double-check these rotations in the code (if I remember correctly this is not the only place where you rotate a size) as I'm not enough into scrcpy's orientation/rotation aspects to really understand my own change (I was just debugging with logs here and there).

body:
At first sight, in the case where `preserveAspectRatio` is `false`, I think this strategy works when the major dimension is the first to reach the encoder's limits, but maybe not when it's the minor dimension that must be constrained.

Imagine the limits are always 500x1000 (max size is zero) and the alignment is 1px. Assume the previous size was 400x800.

On the one hand, if only the width is increased to 600 while the height remains at 800, then `maxBlock = major = height = 800` and `minor = width = 600`. The binary search thus yields a best size of 500x666 in order to respect the highest supported size while preserving the aspect ratio. Final size 500x666 (I would have expected 500x800).

On the other hand, if trying to go to 400x1200, the best major is 1000 (so a size of 333x1000 if the aspect ratio would have to be preserved), however the best minor can extend afterward up to the original 400 since this remains below the limit 500. Final size 400x1000 (as expected).

Put differently:
- when I pull the major dimension beyond its limit, it just saturates to the highest in-bound value as expected (the minor dimension remains untouched);
- when I pull the minor dimension beyond its limit, it correctly saturates at the limit, however it also shrinks the major dimension while I would expect to just keep the previous value in this direction (think of resizing a flex display).

Note that I elaborated this example just mentally so I may be plain wrong. I will have to test how it behaves in reality.

body:
I wondered the same thing this morning, but I think a single step is sufficient.

Initially, we have a supported size; let's call it `(initialBestMajor, associatedBestMinor)`, derived from `bestBlock`.

First, we compute `bestMinor`:

```
bestMinor = computeBestMinorFromMajor(initialBestMajor)
```

Then `bestMajor`:

```
bestMajor = computeBestMajorFromMinor(bestMinor)
```

Since it is the highest possible _major_ (within contraints) associated with `bestMinor` that is supported, it is necessarily greater or equal to the initial best major (`bestMajor >= initialBestMajor`).

If we try to compute again:

```
newBestMinor = computeBestMinorFromMajor(bestMajor)
```

We know that `bestMajor >= initialBestMajor`, so **assuming that the capabilities are consistent** (the smaller the major, the greater the minor, which is a reasonable assumption), it follows that `newBestMinor <= bestMinor` .

Since `(bestMinor, bestMajor)` is a supported size, we know that at least `bestMinor` is supported, so `newBestMinor >= bestMinor`.

Therefore, `newBestMinor == bestMinor`. QED
